model_narrative: We plan a daily diet by choosing how many dollars to spend on each available food. Each food delivers known amounts of various nutrients per dollar spent. We decide the dollar amount to spend on each food. The objective is to make the total daily food bill as small as possible.

sets.name: n
sets.doc: the nutrients that the daily diet must supply

sets.name: f
sets.doc: the foods that can be purchased for the diet

params.name: b
params.doc: the required daily allowance of each nutrient, in that nutrient's own units

params.name: a
params.doc: the amount of a nutrient obtained per dollar spent on a food, in that nutrient's own units per dollar

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the number of dollars spent daily on each food

vars.name: cost
vars.doc: the total daily food bill, in dollars

constraints.name: a[f,
constraints.intent: Every nutrient must be supplied in at least its required daily allowance. For each nutrient, add up the amount of that nutrient delivered by every food given how many dollars are spent on it, and require that total to be at least the required allowance for that nutrient.

constraints.name: cost
constraints.intent: The total daily food bill is the sum of the dollars spent on all the foods. Set the cost variable equal to that total.

query: Our wellness team is pitching a leafy-greens push for the menu. There's a proposal on the table to have cabbage, spinach, green beans, and onions together account for at least 5 cents of daily spend. What does the optimizer return for the food bill once that floor is in?

query: Procurement is uneasy about how grain-heavy the plan looks next to the beans. We're looking at a rule that keeps wheat to no more than a quarter of the combined wheat-plus-navy-bean spend. Plug that in and tell me where the daily cost lands.

query: The kitchen wants more variety so no single item dominates the cart. Engineering is pitching a flat ceiling — no individual food may exceed 3 cents of daily spend, applied across every food on the list. What does the optimizer return for the resulting bill?

query: The whole plan leans hard on the beans — navy beans, lima beans, and peanut butter are doing a lot of the lifting. Shouldn't those three legumes together stay under 3 cents a day so the cart isn't so bean-dominated? What's keeping the model from spreading it out?

query: The greens barely register next to all the wheat in this plan. Wouldn't it be more balanced to spend at least half again as much on leafy greens as on wheat — so cabbage, spinach, green beans, and onions together come to at least one-and-a-half times the wheat spend? What's the tradeoff pulling the optimizer the other way?
expected_pyomo: model.greens_vs_wheat = Constraint(expr=model.x['cabbage'] + model.x['spinach'] + model.x['greenbeans'] + model.x['onions'] >= 1.5 * model.x['wheat'])

query: Liver is a real nutrition powerhouse but the plan keeps it to a sliver — less than the spinach, even. Shouldn't we at least be buying as much liver as spinach each day? Walk me through what's pulling the optimizer toward spinach instead.

query: Two things bug me about this plan at once: there's hardly any greens, and navy beans are carrying way too much of it. Couldn't we insist on at least 3 cents a day across cabbage, spinach, green beans, and onions while also holding navy beans to no more than 4 cents? What's the model seeing that makes it resist both at the same time?
expected_pyomo:
model.greens_floor = Constraint(expr=model.x['cabbage'] + model.x['spinach'] + model.x['greenbeans'] + model.x['onions'] >= 0.03)
model.navybeans_cap = Constraint(expr=model.x['navybeans'] <= 0.04)

query: Couldn't we just anchor the diet on a flat 3 cents a day each of the two cheap grains, wheat and cornmeal, and fill in the rest? What would that simple staple-first plan cost?
